title: Enhanced Contrast Ratios and Rapid Switching in Electrochromics Based on Poly(3,4-propylenedioxythiophene) Derivatives

Enhanced Contrast Ratios and Rapid Switching
in Electrochromics Based on
Poly(3,4-propylenedioxythiophene) Derivatives**
By Dean M. Welsh, Anil Kumar, E. W. Meijer,
and John R. Reynolds*
Electrochromics (ECs) are materials that exhibit varying
colors when held at various potentials.[1] In order to be use-
ful for applications, these materials must exhibit long-term
stability, rapid redox switching, and large changes in trans-
mittance between states (large D%T values). Many inor-
ganic materials have been used as EC materials, but diffi-
culties in processing and slow response times have created
the need for different types of EC materials. Conjugated,
redox-active polymers offer a different approach to EC
materials.[1] These polymers are inherently electrochromic,
and they can be switched electrochemically or chemically
between different colored states.[2] Most of the well-studied
conjugated polymers are anodically coloring, meaning they
are more deeply colored in their oxidized form. Poly(thio-
phene) (PT) and its 3-substituted derivatives fall into this
category. These polymers are red to purple in their reduced
or neutral form, and dark blue to black in their oxidized
form. Although this type of color change can be useful, a
more desirable color change would be one where the poly-
mer switches from a highly colored state to a highly trans-
missive state.
Of the conducting polymers available, poly(3,4-ethylene-
dioxythiophene) (PEDOT) and its derivatives exhibit the
most promising EC properties. When compared to PT,
PEDOT exhibits more rapid switching, lower oxidation po-
tentials, and greater stability at ambient and elevated tem-
peratures.[3,4] In addition, PEDOT is a cathodically coloring
polymer that is a dark opaque blue in its reduced form, and
a very transmissive light blue in its oxidized form.[5±7] In
our laboratory, we have successfully exploited the advan-
tages of EDOT to synthesize many EC polymers.[8±10] We
have shown that PEDOT can be used as a cathodically col-
oring polymer in dual-polymer EC devices with a D%T of
45 % at 620 nm.[11] Although other laboratories have stud-
ied PEDOT and other alkylenedioxythiophene deriva-
tives,[7,12] our laboratory recently published the first exhaus-
tive study of electrical, optical, and conductive properties
of a large series of alkylenedioxythiophene derivatives.[13]
We found that the D%T value of PEDOT can be improved
significantly by increasing the alkylenedioxythiophene ring
size or by incorporating substituents.
Of the derivatives synthesized, poly(3,4-propylenedioxy-
thiophene) (PProDOT) exhibited the greatest maximum
in-situ conductivity, and because of its structure, it can be
symmetrically disubstituted on the central carbon of the
propylene bridge.[13] Our previous synthetic route did not
allow for this disubstitution, but now we report the first di-
substituted derivative, PProDOT±Me2. PProDOT±Me2 ex-
hibits an extremely high D%T value of 78 % at lmax
(578 nm), in fact the highest reported to date. We report
here its optical properties, and compare them to PProDOT
and the mono-substituted derivative, PProDOT±Me.
The monomer synthesis is shown in Schemes 1a and 1b.
ProDOT and ProDOT±Me were synthesized using the
route illustrated in Scheme 1a, with the key step being a
Williamson etherification reaction between 1 and the ap-
propriate dibromo or ditosyl compound.[4,13] This reaction
proceeds well for ProDOT and ProDOT±Me, but in the
case of ProDOT±Me2 the substitution would have to pro-
ceed on a neopentyl-type structure, which is known to be
difficult, and no reaction was observed when this method
was attempted. Fortunately, ProDOT±Me2 was successfully
synthesized using the transetherification reaction illus-
trated in Scheme 1b.[14,15] This reaction proceeds in 56 %
yield, and its success opens the door to the possibility of
many new symmetrically substituted derivatives. All mono-
mers were characterized by 1H and 13C NMR spectroscopy,
high-resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS), and elemental
analysis.
For spectroelectrochemical studies, polymer films were
synthesized on indium tin oxide (ITO) coated glass slides
from a 10 mM solution of monomer in 0.1 M tetrabutylam-
monium perchlorate/acetonitrile (TBAP/ACN) at a con-

stant potential of 1.2 V vs. Ag/
Ag+. The films were washed with
monomer-free
electrolyte
solu-
tion, and a series of UV-vis-NIR
spectra were obtained at various
potentials. In order to illustrate
the high contrast of these materi-
als, only the spectra of the fully
oxidized and the fully reduced
forms are shown in Figure 1a (for
PProDOT) and Figure 1b (for
PProDOT±Me2).
Although PProDOT and PPro-
DOT±Me2 both show good con-
trast in the visible region, PPro-
DOT±Me2
shows
a
higher
contrast. This higher contrast is
due to its larger percentage trans-
mittance in the oxidized form, a
consequence
of
its
attenuated
peak in the NIR region. Because of the dimethyl substitu-
tion, interchain charge movement is proposed to be hin-
dered, and the conductivity of the sample is lowered. This
results in attenuation of the typically large NIR absorption
for highly conducting samples, and in larger transparency
in the visible region. Because of the apparent high contrast
in the spectroelectrochemistry, optical switching studies
were performed to more deeply probe the polymer's opti-
cal properties.
For optical switching studies, polymer films were synthe-
sized on ITO-coated glass slides in the same manner as de-
scribed above. The films were washed with ACN and dried.
The film thickness (as determined by profilometry) versus
charge density was determined for each polymer system,
and in each case the thickness scaled linearly with charge
density, allowing us to control film thickness. For the stud-
ies, each polymer was grown to four different thicknesses
from 100 to 250 nm, and each film was potential stepped
between its reduced (±1.0 V) and oxidized (+1.0 V) state.
While the films were switched, the percentage transmit-
tance (at 578 nm) was monitored as a function of time with
UV-vis-NIR spectroscopy. The contrast is given as the dif-
ference in transmittance between the oxidized and reduced
states and reported as D%T. Switching data for PProDOT
and PProDOT±Me2 of films with the thickness that pro-
duced maximum D%T are given in Figures 2a and 2b, re-
spectively.
As expected from the spectroelectrochemistry, PPro-
DOT±Me2 shows a D%T of close to 78 %, which is signifi-
cantly higher than the 62 % of PProDOT, or the 45 % of
PEDOT.[13] To the best of our knowledge, this value consti-
tutes the highest contrast reported in the literature to date
for an EC polymer. PProDOT±Me also shows a high con-
trast of 77 % at optimum film thickness. Switching speeds
are also important, and each of these polymers switches
very rapidly. The measured switching times, at 95 % of full
contrast for varying film thickness, range from 0.3 to 0.8 s.
For comparison purposes, PEDOT films of similar thick-
ness switch in approximately 2 s while poly(3-methyl thio-
Scheme 1.
Fig. 1. Optical spectrum of PProDOT (a) and PProDOT±Me2 (b) in the
fully oxidized and reduced forms.

As expected from the spectroelectrochemistry, PPro-
DOT±Me2 shows a D%T of close to 78 %, which is signifi-
cantly higher than the 62 % of PProDOT, or the 45 % of
PEDOT.[13] To the best of our knowledge, this value consti-
tutes the highest contrast reported in the literature to date
for an EC polymer. PProDOT±Me also shows a high con-
trast of 77 % at optimum film thickness. Switching speeds
are also important, and each of these polymers switches
very rapidly. The measured switching times, at 95 % of full
contrast for varying film thickness, range from 0.3 to 0.8 s.
For comparison purposes, PEDOT films of similar thick-
ness switch in approximately 2 s while poly(3-methyl thio-
Fig. 1. Optical spectrum of PProDOT (a) and PProDOT±Me2 (b) in the
fully oxidized and reduced forms.
phene) films switch in 4 s with much lower contrasts.[16] In
the family reported here, PProDOT showed the slowest
switch, followed by PProDOT±Me, and then PProDOT±
Me2 as the fastest. This trend is not surprising, as more
highly substituted polymers should polymerize to a more
open morphology, therefore allowing for faster ion move-
ment, which is often the rate-limiting process, during redox
Coloration efficiencies (CEs) have been used extensively
in EC materials to allow for comparison of devices contain-
ing different types of materials. The CE is obtained by de-
termining the injected/ejected charge as a function of elec-
trode area (Qd) and the change in optical density DOD(l)
when switching an EC device from one state to another,
and is given by Equation 1, where DOD(l) = log[Tb(l)/
CE values were calculated at varying film thickness for
the polymers presented here as films on ITO in solution,
and all gave values of approximately 200 cm2/C. It should
be noted that EC metal oxides typically exhibit CEs rang-
ing from 10 to 100 cm2/C, while values observed for EC
polymers tend to be higher. Although the CE value of a
high-quality EC device can reach into the 1000 cm2/C
range, the results for these devices cannot be compared to
To conclude, new disubstituted PProDOT derivatives
can be synthesized, and we are currently working on
synthesizing polymers with longer alkyl chains for soluble
derivatives. PProDOT±Me and PProDOT±Me2 exhibit
much higher contrast and more rapid switching times than
the parent PProDOT. These results support our earlier
conclusions that increasing substitution on these polymer
systems leads to higher contrasts and faster switching. A
more detailed study of these derivatives, and a comparison
with more conventional conducting polymers will follow.
3,3-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydro-2H-thieno[3,4-b][1,4]dioxepine (ProDOT±Me2):
To a three-necked flask equipped with a nitrogen purge was added 3 g
(20.8 mmol) of 3,4-dimethoxythiophene, 21.6 g (208 mmol) of 2,2-dimethyl-
1,3-propanediol, 0.4 g (2.08 mmol) of p-toluenesulfonic acid monohydrate,
and 30 mL of toluene. The solution was heated overnight at 80 C. The result-
ing mixture was filtered, the toluene-soluble fraction was washed with water,
and the toluene was removed under vacuum to yield a green viscous oil. The
oil was purified by column chromatography using silica gel and 2:3 methylene
chloride/heptane as eluent to yield 2.1 g (56 %) of white product (m.p. 49±
51 C). 1H NMR (CDCl3) d 1.02 (s, 6H), 3.73 (s, 4H), 6.48 (s, 2H); 13C NMR
(CDCl3) d 22.25, 39.44, 80.62, 105.09, 150.05; Elemental analysis: Calcd. for
C9H12O2S: C, 58.67%; H, 6.56 %; S, 17.40 %. Found: C, 58.81%; H, 6.76 %;
S, 16.98%; HRMS calcd. for C9H12O2S: 184.0558. Found: 184.0561.
Electrochemistry: Electrochemical studies were carried out using an
EG&G Model 273A potentiostat/galvanostat with Ag/Ag+ as the reference
electrode, an ITO-coated glass slide as the working electrode, and stainless
steel or Pt as the counter electrode in 0.1 M TBAP/ACN. Spectroelectro-
chemistry was carried out on a Varian Cary 5E UV-vis-NIR spectrophoto-
meter. Film thicknesses were measured using a Sloan Dektak II profilo-

Near IR and UV Enhanced Photoresponse
of C60-Doped Semiconducting Polymer
By Katsumi Yoshino,* Sergey Lee, Akihiko Fujii,
Hiroaki Nakayama, Wolfgang Schneider, Akinobu Naka,
Polymeric photodiodes and photovoltaic devices based
on p-conjugated polymers have attracted much attention in
recent years. Since the discovery of efficient photoinduced
charge transfer between conducting polymers and C60
much work has been devoted to the application of this do-
nor±acceptor system in a high sensitivity photodiode and
effective photovoltaic cell.[3±6] Excellent sensitivity in the
visible and near ultraviolet (UV) spectral range has been
The thienylene-based oligomers present very interesting
properties can be controlled by changing the conjugation
length of the well part and also the barrier part.[8] On the
other hand, polymers with the regular alternating arrange-
ments of a Si±Si bond and p-electron system in a main
chain attracted much attention because they can be used as
conducting and photoconducting materials[9±11] as well as
the top imaging layer for the double layer photoresist sys-
tem.[12] The polymers are soluble in common organic sol-
vents such as benzene, ether, and halocarbons, but the
solubility decreased markedly with increasing number of
the thienylene rings per disilanylene unit. However, intro-
duction of the longer alkyl groups onto a silicon atom af-
forded a soluble polymer. Recent study has shown that op-
tical properties of poly[(disilanylene)-(oligothienylene)m]
are dependent on the length (m) of the oligothienylene
units.[11] Their stability is much higher than polysilanylene
and increases with increasing length of oligothienylene.
These polymers possess a relatively high hole mobility in
the order of 10±4 cm2V±1s±1 because of probable s±p conju-
gation between the oligothienylenes and Si±Si units.[9]
In this communication we present the results of a study
of a semiconducting polymer photodiode fabricated from
the new C60-doped semiconducting polymer, poly[(tetra-
butyldisilanylene)pentakis(thienylene)] (PDS(Th)5). The
high spectral sensitivity extended far below the absorption
edge into the near infrared (IR) and has also been found in
the UV region for the Al/C60-doped PDS(Th)5/ITO hetero-
structure. The electronic energy band scheme of the
heterostructure ITO/C60-doped PDS(Th)5/Al is presented.
The same pristine polymer shows a strong photo- and elec-
Figure 1a shows the absorption spectra of pristine and
C60-doped (5 mol.-%) PDS(Th)5. Four absorption bands
centered at 420 nm (I), 310 nm (II), 264 nm (III), and
³190 nm (IV) have been observed in the pristine polymer
(dotted line). Band III is independent of the conjugation
length of the thienylene unit.[11] It should be noted that
only three absorption bands (I, III, and IV) have been ob-
served in non-atomically-bridged substituted polythienyl-
enes.[1,13] Band II, at 310 nm, lies too far from the first ab-
sorption band to be a phonon replica. To our knowledge, in
contrast to PPV theoretical quantum-chemical calculations,
UV absorption has not yet been reported for polythienyl-
enes and oligothienylenes. However, it should be noted
that there is a resemblance of the absorption spectrum of
PDS(Th)5 to that of the substituted phenylene-based conju-
gated polymers.[14] Similar spectra have been theoretically
obtained for the atomically bridged polyphenylenes with
the broken charge-conjugation symmetry[15] although it has
been shown that the effect of the broken charge-conjuga-
tion symmetry has an almost insignificant effect on poly-
mer absorption. The origin of band II is mainly due to the
finite-size effect,[14] since the real conjugated polymers con-
sist of short conjugated segments. In this communication
Three characteristic absorption bands of the C60 mol-
ecule with maxima at 216 nm, 262 nm, and 340 nm in the
UV region and slight broadening of the main absorption
band of the polymer after C60 doping are clearly seen in
the absorption spectrum of C60-doped PDS(Th)5 (Fig. 1a,
solid line). The absorption band of C60 at 262 nm overlaps
band III of the polymer, which makes it difficult to sepa-
rate the absorption effects of the polymer and C60.
The C60-doped PDS(Th)5 is stable to oxidation. Its stabil-
ity is higher than that of polysilanes and most p-conjugated
polymers. However, the bleaching effect and a drastic de-
crease in photoconductivity were observed upon intense
UV-irradiation with a NIKON super high pressure mercury
lamp (model HB-10101AF) in air over a period of 0.5±1 h.
